mnemonics.title: Hazardous E-Waste Elements
mnemonics.content: LEAD CRUBS - Lead, Cadmium, Mercury, Ruthenium, Beryllium, Antimony, Selenium (common toxic elements in e-waste)

mnemonics.title: 3R Principle
mnemonics.content: REDUCE before REUSE, then RECYCLE - Priority order for waste management hierarchy

acronyms.usage: Makes manufacturers responsible for end-of-life collection and recycling of their products
Discarded electrical and electronic devices at end-of-life
Regulatory body that authorizes e-waste recyclers in India
Collection model where collectors pick up e-waste from households
Rules that mandate e-waste segregation from municipal waste
Common plastic in electronic cables that releases dioxins when burned
Common plastic in electronic device casings

visualTips.visualization: Draw an inverted pyramid: Top = Reduce (smallest but most important), Middle = Reuse, Bottom = Recycle, Base = Dispose (last resort)
Picture a computer motherboard: Red lines = copper, Yellow dots = gold, Silver areas = silver, Green = lead solder, Blue = mercury in batteries
Draw arrows: Consumer → Collection Center → Sorting → Dismantling → Separation → Recycling → Raw Material → Manufacturing

analogies.analogy: Just like traditional mining extracts ores from earth, urban mining extracts precious metals from discarded electronics - our 'mines' are our garbage bins!
Like a book that gets passed to different readers (reuse) before becoming paper pulp (recycle), electronics should pass through multiple users before material recovery.
Extended Producer Responsibility is like a restaurant being responsible for cleaning up after their disposable plates are thrown away, not just the customer.
